root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12 is unroutable (R1->R2 path exists but R2 cannot send replies back).
evidence: R1 routing: has route for 172.16.0.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1 routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list entry: {"prefix": "172.16.0.0", "fwd-list": [{"fwd": "10.0.0.2"}]}).
R1 Serial0/1/0 IP: 10.0.0.1/30 (connected to R2).
R2 Serial0/1/0 IP: 10.0.0.2/30 (connected to R1).

R2 routing: no specific route for 192.168.0.0/24 — routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list contains only a default route ({"prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{"fwd": "10.0.0.6"}]}) and no entry for 192.168.0.0/24.

Ping from R2 to R1 (10.0.0.1) succeeded: "Success rate is 100 percent (5/5), round-trip min/avg/max = 2/2/3 ms".
remaining_issues: Access-list R2_TO_R1 on R2 includes an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443; this may block HTTPS traffic between the LANs and should be validated as intentional.
End-to-end host verification (e.g., ping/traceroute or an application test from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12) has not been shown — recommend one host-to-host test to confirm full connectivity.

messages.content:
What was observed
- R2 had no route for 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12 was unroutable.

Diagnosis (main cause)
- Missing return route on R2: R1->R2 path existed, but R2 had no specific route to 192.168.0.0/24.

Changes made
- Added a static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1) — installed on Serial0/1/0.

Verification
- Passed: R2 running-config contains the new route; ping from R2 to R1 (10.0.0.1) succeeded 100% (5/5).
- Remaining: ACL "R2_TO_R1" on R2 contains an explicit deny for TCP from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (may block HTTPS). Recommend an end-to-end host test (ping/traceroute or application test from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12) to confirm full connectivity.
